purpose: A multi-axis scenario taxonomy for screenplay creation. It treats scenarios as factorized lenses, not a single canon.
classification_principles: Use the smallest set of axes that explains the request well enough to route it.
No single axis is authoritative on its own.
Axes are lenses for routing and comparison, not a fixed truth table.
A scenario may combine multiple cards when the task is genuinely hybrid.

axes.label: Medium
axes.purpose: What container the script must serve.

axes.label: Format Pressure
axes.purpose: What shape the deliverable must take.

axes.label: Audience Need State
axes.purpose: What the audience wants to feel, solve, or receive.

axes.label: Emotional Contract
axes.purpose: What emotional promise the work is making.

axes.label: Production Context
axes.purpose: What industrial condition is shaping the script.

axes.label: Participation Mode
axes.purpose: How the audience interacts with the content.

axes.label: Business Goal
axes.purpose: What the script is expected to help achieve.

axes.label: Genre Pressure
axes.purpose: What genre promise is most active.

axes.label: Source Relation
axes.purpose: How strongly the work is tied to existing source material.

axes.label: Collaboration Mode
axes.purpose: How the work is being developed socially.

axes.label: Risk Profile
axes.purpose: How sensitive or constrained the scenario is.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Original feature concepts that must hold attention over a long arc.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: He keeps the fire map in his jacket and walks back into the warehouse after the city declares the collapse contained.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: A man has a problem, learns something, and goes home changed.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It gives the protagonist a concrete object, a visible risk, and a pressure source outside the character.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It states theme without a dramatic mechanism that can carry scenes.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A different route can still work if the project is a chamber piece or a mood-led film.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Series pilots and recurring-episode engines that need repeatable story motion.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: Every Monday the mediator opens the rooftop clinic waiting list while the building's power fails again.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The show has interesting people who can have new problems every week.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It makes the series engine repeatable and shows the weekly pressure source.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It promises variety without specifying the system that generates episodes.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some series should be seasonal and cumulative rather than weekly and repeatable.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Case-of-the-week stories where each episode must close cleanly but still feed the series.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The body is solved by dawn, but the final clue points to a clerk who never appears on camera.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: There is a mystery and then the team solves it in a satisfying way.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The case has closure and a tail that keeps the audience returning.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It describes the effect instead of the mechanism.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A procedural can still feel lyrical, political, or character-first.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Short-form drama that must hook fast and escalate before the scroll resumes.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: She reads the message, smiles once, deletes it, and opens the door anyway.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The episode slowly introduces the characters and eventually something surprising happens.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The hook arrives immediately and the action reverses the apparent emotional state.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It spends too long warming up for a format that punishes delay.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some short drama succeeds through atmosphere or repetition, not only twist density.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Animated stories where the world rule must create both play and conflict.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: By sunrise, any lie left in the village becomes a visible stain on the speaker's shadow.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The world is imaginative and full of colorful characters.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The rule creates visual storytelling and social pressure at the same time.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It describes style but not a dramatic system.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Animation can also work by realism, restraint, or emotional intimacy.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Thrillers that must keep the audience making and revising hypotheses.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The key he stole at noon opens a room that no one in the building admits exists.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The story is tense and keeps you guessing until the end.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It gives the plot a tangible mystery object and a hidden space with consequences.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It names the effect but not the chain of reveals that produces it.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Not every thriller should be puzzle-first; some are tension-first or character-first.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Romance stories where mutual recognition, not just attraction, must drive the arc.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: He corrects her joke in public, then repeats it exactly the way she meant it when the room empties.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: They fall in love after a series of cute moments.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The scene turns from friction to recognition without losing character voice.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It compresses the entire relationship into a generic summary.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some romances work through slow burn, mismatch, or structural irony rather than direct recognition.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Comedy scenes where setup and payoff must stay visible inside the same beat window.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: He prints the apology memo, hands it to the wrong boss, and accidentally gets promoted for honesty.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The scene has jokes and everyone is funny.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The setup is concrete and the payoff reverses status in a readable way.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It describes tone, not comic mechanics.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Comedy can also be deadpan, tragic, or character-bound instead of setup-payoff dominant.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Prestige or author-driven drama where ambiguity is part of the payoff, not a defect.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: At the dinner table nobody says the word 'war', but every plate is cleared by someone who no longer belongs there.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The story is subtle and meaningful, and the audience should think about it afterward.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It preserves ambiguity while still giving the scene a visible dramatic action.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It relies on prestige language without a concrete dramatic charge.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Ambiguity works only when causality and pressure still remain legible.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Ads that need one message, one behavior, and one visible reason to act now.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: One sip, one switch: the commuter finishes his sentence without rubbing his eyes for the first time all day.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Our product has many great features and a friendly brand voice.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The product benefit is visible in a single action and the story remains message-dense.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It becomes a feature dump with no decisive behavioral outcome.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some campaigns need emotional brand building instead of immediate conversion.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Brand films that need identity and value to emerge through story, not slogan.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: She drives through the flood tunnel not to escape the storm, but to arrive with the tools to rebuild the street.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The brand stands for resilience, innovation, and caring about people.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The brand value is dramatized through an action that reveals identity.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It announces the value without making it felt.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A branded film can be quiet, funny, documentary-like, or highly stylized.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Vertical or short-form social scripts where the first second matters.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: She holds the broken blender to camera, presses one button, and the kitchen goes quiet instead of loud.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Let me tell you about a product that could improve your routine.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The proof arrives before explanation and the visual beat is legible instantly.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It spends the hook budget on setup instead of payoff.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some short-form pieces win through intrigue or charisma rather than instant utility.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Game narratives that need repeatable play loops and story consequences.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: Each relic you return changes the camp, but each camp upgrade closes one road and opens another.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The player goes on quests and learns more about the world.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The quest loop changes state and creates tradeoffs, which keeps play meaningful.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It describes content without a system.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some games are built around exploration, survival, or social systems instead of quests.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Interactive stories where choices must alter state, not just dialogue flavor.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: If she lies, the guard believes her; if she tells the truth, the guard remembers her face forever.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The player makes interesting choices and sees different scenes.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The choice changes future state and therefore future story pressure.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It gives variety without consequence.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Branching can be shallow, deep, or mostly social; consequence design should match the actual product.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Adaptations that must preserve core conflict while changing container and audience contract.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The novel's secret is not the missing letter; it is who keeps rewriting the letter after each death.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: We should keep the source material accurate and try to be faithful.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It identifies the core engine to preserve and the container logic to translate.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It praises fidelity without deciding what fidelity means.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some adaptations should be close, others should be radical; fidelity is a strategy, not a moral law.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Diagnosis work where the first job is to identify the failing layer.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The first act is not weak because it is slow; it is weak because the protagonist has no decision that changes the board.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The draft is bad and needs to be improved.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The diagnosis names the layer, the symptom, and the missing function.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It evaluates without localizing the failure.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A rewrite note can prioritize structure, character, tone, or dialogue depending on the actual failure layer.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: Dialogue passes where voice, subtext, and power dynamics need to separate cleanly.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: A: You came late. B: I came when it mattered. A: That is not the same thing, and you know it.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: A: I am upset. B: I understand that you are upset.
scenario_cards.why_strong: The exchange has asymmetry, subtext, and immediate relational pressure.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It says the emotion instead of making it audible.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some scenes should be sparse and some should be talky; voice quality matters more than line count.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the question is not what to write, but who this is for and whether it matches the ask.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: This version serves urban streaming viewers who want suspense plus emotional release, but it risks losing audiences who need broader catharsis.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: The audience will probably like this because it is good.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It names the audience segment, the need state, and the fit risk.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It uses taste language instead of an evidence-backed fit statement.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Audience fit is a useful lens, not a command to flatten ambition into the safest possible output.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the problem is development strategy, not line-level execution.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The project should keep the core conflict, move the reveal earlier, and reserve the deepest twist for the second-quarter restart.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: We should make the project stronger and more marketable.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It connects creative direction to production and release logic.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It sounds strategic but does not make the next decision easier.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A development brief should sharpen choices, not pre-solve every creative detail.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the user needs a growth path rather than a single answer.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: Week 1 writes loglines, week 2 writes scene functions, week 3 rewrites two scenes for different audience need states.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Practice writing every day and read good scripts.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It turns improvement into staged, testable practice.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It gives advice without a learning loop.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Learning paths should adapt to the writer's current gap, not impose a generic curriculum.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the real problem is how a screenplay task should be organized across specialists, handoffs, and review gates.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: Run season-arc, episode-break, and continuity lanes in parallel, then force all three back through a showrunner synthesis gate before script drafting.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Use multiple agents to make the work faster.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It specifies roles, cadence, and gate logic rather than treating parallelism as a virtue by itself.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It confuses concurrency with a usable workflow architecture.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some screenplay tasks are still better handled by one focused route; team mode is for genuine collaboration pressure, not for every request.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When a draft needs explicit voice anchors before scene, dialogue, commercial, or continuity work can proceed safely.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: He jokes early, but the joke always covers status anxiety; the guide should preserve that move without forcing the exact wording.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Make him sound more natural, sarcastic, and human.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It defines voice through behavior, pressure, and repeatable drift checks instead of vague adjectives.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It names a tone without giving the agent any usable constraint or continuity anchor.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: A voice guide should reduce drift, not freeze every future line into the same pattern.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the right answer is not one route but several materially different bets.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: Path A centers the relationship, Path B centers the mystery, Path C centers the social system; each wins by a different promise.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: Here are three versions of the same idea with different wording.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It compares genuinely different bets instead of packaging the same one three ways.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It manufactures variety without changing the underlying tradeoff.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Some tasks should converge fast; path options are a tool for uncertainty, not a requirement everywhere.

scenario_cards.when_to_use: When the task is to separate hard-no zones, soft-risk zones, and a narrowed surviving core.
scenario_cards.reference_fragment: The rule is not wrong; it is too broad. In festival dramas it survives, in brand work it does not, and the safe zone sits between them.
scenario_cards.weak_fragment: This rule is good in some cases and bad in others.
scenario_cards.why_strong: It gives an explicit boundary map and preserves a surviving core instead of treating the disagreement as all-or-nothing.
scenario_cards.why_weak: It stays vague about what survives and where it breaks.
scenario_cards.non_dogma_note: Boundary mapping and scope correction should protect creative room, not erase it.

usage_notes: Treat the axes as a stack, not as a ranking of truth.
Use the scenario cards as reference patterns, not mandatory templates.
If a request is hybrid, keep both dominant scenarios visible until the route genuinely collapses.
If a rule is challenged, ask whether it needs a boundary map, a scope correction, or a path split before you rewrite it.
